testcase: Delete operation status code 200 deprecated
query:
title: Delete operation status code 200 deprecated

question: Hi, 
We just experienced this in a new API where we followed [the guideline](https://github.com/cloud-and-ai-microsoft/resource-provider-contract/blob/master/v1.0/delete-resource.md#response) to implement the operation, and it failed in the SDK handling of the response.
 
our implementation is an async delete operation. but sometimes, the delete can be performed synchronously. it depends on the state of the system.
 
async delete: 202 + LRO header (cleanup happening on async side)
sync delete: 200 (no cleanup needed)
already gone 204
 
Why is that not appropriate?
Should the guideline be updated? this is not the first time that I report discrepancies between Typespec and the official RPC guidelines.
Could you make sure that ensuring the guideline is aligned is included in typespec development cycle?
ground_truth:
The RPC rule is pretty explicit about this:
```
Delete APIs must define the following status codes. For synchronous APIs - 200 and 204 must be specified. For asynchronous APIs - 202 and 204 must be specified.
```
In other words, mixing synchronous and asynchronous delete is not recommended.
This is from the text of RPC-Delete-V1-01 from the RPC page you specified.  I agree that the explanatory text above this is a little badly worded, so it could be interpreted either way.  The rules listed below the explanatory text are meant to be definitive in these cases.
Note that there is now a doc that correlates RPC rules and how they are mapped into typespec rules here: [ARM RPC Guidelines and TypeSpec Linting Coverage | TypeSpec Azure](https://azure.github.io/typespec-azure/docs/howtos/arm/rpc-guidelines-coverage/)

expected_references.title: ARM RPC Guidelines and TypeSpec Linting Coverage | TypeSpec Azure

testcase: MadeOptional decorator
Custom annotation support
query:
title: Custom annotation support

question: Hi team, we would like to implement a custom annotation to help us mark, in TypeSpec, which version a given property was introduced in, so that it can facilitate future stable version releases. After implementing this, the local build succeeds, but the remote pipeline detects it and fails. 
Therefore, we would like to understand whether such custom annotations are supported.
```
@introducedIn("2023-11-01-preview")
@added(Versions.vLatestPreview)
retryProvisioning is ArmResourceActionSync<SessionHost, void, OkResponse>;
```

```
1  + const introducedInKey = Symbol.for("introducedIn");
2  +
3  + /**
4  +  * Mark when a field or operation was introduced in a specific API version
5  +  * @param context - The decorator context
6  +  * @param target - The target entity (field, operation, interface, etc.)
7  +  * @param version - The API version when this was introduced (e.g., "2023-11-01-preview")
8  +  */
9  + export function $introducedIn(context, target, version) {
10 +   // Store the version information in the program state
11 +   context.program.stateMap(introducedInKey).set(target, version);
12 + }
13 +
14 + /**
15 +  * Get the introduced version for an entity
16 +  * @param program - The TypeSpec program
17 +  * @param target - The target entity
18 +  * @returns The version string or undefined
19 +  */
20 + export function getIntroducedVersion(program, target) {
21 +   return program.stateMap(introducedInKey).get(target);
22 + }
```

```
specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2627:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2627 | @introducedIn("2023-11-01-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2634:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2634 | @introducedIn("2025-08-01-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2641:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2641 | @introducedIn("2025-08-01-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2167:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2167 | @introducedIn("2023-10-04-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2237:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2237 | @introducedIn("2023-10-04-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2244:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2244 | @introducedIn("2025-03-01-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

specification/desktopvirtualization/resource-manager/Microsoft.DesktopVirtualization/DesktopVirtualization/models.tsp:2310:4 - error invalid-ref: Unknown decorator @introducedIn
> 2310 | @introducedIn("2025-08-01-preview")
       | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```
ground_truth:
Custom decorators are supported by the TypeSpec language and will compile locally, but the Azure REST API validation pipeline has additional constraints and is not guaranteed to accept arbitrary, service‑specific decorators today.
In practice:
You can use your own decorator in your TypeSpec project to record metadata (like the version in which a property was introduced), and the local tsp compile will succeed.
However, the remote validation pipeline (in azure-rest-api-specs) currently expects specs to stick to the approved Azure TypeSpec libraries and patterns. A custom decorator that only carries metadata and is not part of the standard/azure libraries can be flagged by those checks, even if it doesn’t change the generated OpenAPI.
For marking when a property or operation was introduced, you should use the official versioning decorators from @typespec/versioning (and the Azure guidance on top of that): @added, @removed, @renamedFrom, @madeOptional, etc. Those are the supported way to express “this property first appears in version X” and are understood both by emitters and by the Azure tooling.
Given your pipeline failure, the recommended approach is:
1.Remove or rename your custom version-introduction decorator so it is not used directly in the specs submitted to azure-rest-api-specs.
2.Replace its usage with the standard versioning decorators, for example:
```
model MyModel {
  @added(Versions.`2025-07-01-preview`)
  myNewProp?: string;
}
```
3.If you still want a custom annotation for internal tooling, implement it in a separate internal TypeSpec library and ensure it does not appear in the specs you submit to the public repo (e.g., strip it as part of your internal build, or only use it in internal branches).
If you share the exact diagnostic from the pipeline, we can confirm precisely which rule is rejecting the decorator and suggest a compliant pattern, but conceptually the pipeline is expecting you to rely on the built‑in @added/@removed‑style versioning rather than a custom version marker.

testcase: Breaking Change Review Help
query:
title: Breaking Change Review Help

question: I have made changes to some typespec models where I have added the annotation @removed on them for the new api version I have introduced. Now I am getting the "Breaking Change" label on my PR preventing it from getting reviewed. The RTs that are marked with @removed are not being used by the service/customers any more. So what is the guidance for proceeding with this scenario?
ground_truth:
You’re hitting the expected cross‑version breaking‑change checks (1005 RemovedPath, 1006 RemovedDefinition). Removing operations/models between GA API versions is a real breaking change from the tooling’s perspective, even if the service no longer uses them.
For management‑plane specs in the private repo, the correct way to proceed is:
1.Decide if this is truly a breaking change vs. a spec bug fix
If the old paths/RTs never worked as described (or have been non‑functional for their whole lifetime), you can treat the change as a bug fix to align spec with actual behavior.
If they were functional and customers could have used them, this is a real breaking change and must follow the formal Breaking Change process.
2.If it’s a spec bug fix (preferred when valid)
You may keep the new API version 2026‑03‑01 and:
Clearly document in the PR description that you are correcting the spec to match the actual service behavior and that the removed paths/models were never usable/never supported for customers.
Apply the label BreakingChange-Approved-BugFix yourself (PR authors are allowed to do this in this scenario).
Re‑run pipelines (/azp run). The BreakingChangeReviewRequired gating is then considered satisfied, and the PR can proceed to ARM review and normal approval.
3.If it’s a real breaking change (customers could have used those APIs)
You must go through the Azure Breaking Change process:
Follow the guidance at https://aka.ms/brch and the Azure Versioning and Breaking Changes Policy (aka.ms/AzBreakingChangesPolicy).
Submit a breaking‑change intake and obtain approval from the Breaking Change Review Board (and API board/SDK board if there’s SDK impact).
Once approved, the breaking‑change approver will apply Approved-BreakingChange (or guide you which approval label to use), which unblocks the PR.
4.About suppressions
For the Breaking Change(Cross-Version) check, there is no permanent suppression path documented; the supported route is policy/board approval plus an appropriate BreakingChange-* label, not a technical suppression in the spec.
So, in your concrete case:
If you can justify that schemaReferences / dynamicSchemas paths and their related RTs were never actually usable or supported, treat this as a bug‑fix, state that explicitly in the PR, and add BreakingChange-Approved-BugFix (and rerun checks).
If they were previously functional, you’ll need to file a breaking‑change proposal via aka.ms/brch, wait for approval, then proceed once an approver has applied the appropriate Approved-BreakingChange label.

testcase: Adding optional request body to existing operation that was previously void?
Committing TypeSpec for existing service
query:
title: Committing TypeSpec for existing service

question: I have a PR. 
After a journey, we believe we're at the point to commit the TypeSpec for the Fast Transcription features of the Speech Service, whose existing swagger.
All 4 SDK's are in in beta, and have been generated from this TypeSpec.
What is our next step to get signoff?
ground_truth:
Given that:
You now have a TypeSpec aligned with the existing Swagger for Fast Transcription, and
All four Tier‑1 SDKs have already been generated in beta from that TypeSpec,
you’re effectively at the SDK Architecture Review phase.
For Azure SDK signoff/approval, the next steps are:
1.Confirm API spec approval
Ensure the TypeSpec has been fully reviewed/approved by the appropriate API board (API Stewardship Board for data plane). That’s the prerequisite before final SDK signoff.
2.If this is your first beta release for these SDKs → request a Beta (Preview) SDK Architecture Review:
Make sure:
You’ve had your Service Introduction meeting with the SDK Architecture Board.
API specs are finalized (your TypeSpec PR is effectively in that state).
At least one SDK (you have four) is ready for review.
CI checks are passing for all libraries in scope.
Then follow the "Beta (Preview) Review" flow from the SDK review doc (email azsdkarch‑help@microsoft.com per that guidance, including APIView links, samples, etc.).
This gives you design signoff for the first beta.
3.When you’re preparing for GA of these SDKs → request a Stable (GA) SDK Architecture Review:
Before emailing azsdkarch‑help@microsoft.com:
Prepare required materials for GA review:
APIView links for all Tier 1 languages (.NET, Java, JS/TS, Python) with attached code samples for hero scenarios.
Link to the API spec directory with client.tsp.
Originating SDK PRs for each APIView.
README links for all Tier 1 libraries.
CI validation passing.
Use the Stable review email template from the docs to request the review.
The architects will review async; when all feedback is addressed you get GA signoff.
4.Create and follow Release Plans for each release:
For the beta and then GA releases, create release plans in Release Planner.
Use the plan to: generate SDK (if you ever need regeneration from TypeSpec), track SDK PRs, and later drive the Release SDK step.
5.After GA signoff, complete the release:
For each language, use the release pipelines as described under "Release SDK" (data-plane, on‑demand).
Ensure you’ve had a beta in market for at least a month before promoting to stable, unless you’ve obtained an exception.
In summary: your immediate next formal SDK step is requesting an SDK Architecture Review (Beta now if this is the first preview signoff, and later a Stable review before GA), using the process and email templates in the SDK review documentation, and coordinating releases via Release Planner.

testcase: npx tsv auto deleting examples from swagger
query:
title: npx tsv auto deleting examples from swagger

question: npx tsv is auto deleting our examples from swagger. We ran validate-example command and validation passes correctly.
```
c:\azurecompute\azure-rest-api-specs\specification\azurefleet\resource-manager\Microsoft.AzureFleet\AzureFleet>oav validate-example "c:\azurecompute\azure-rest-api-specs\specification\azurefleet\resource-manager\Microsoft.AzureFleet\AzureFleet\preview\2026-04-01-preview\azurefleet.json"
{
  message: validating examples under "c:\azurecompute\azure-rest-api-specs\specification\azurefleet\resource-manager\Microsoft.AzureFleet\AzureFleet\preview\2026-04-01-preview\..."
  level: "\x18[32minfo\x18[39m'
}
Validation completes without errors.
```
But when npx tsv is run locally, examples are being deleted from swagger. How do we fix this? We checked that the generated names are correct in the example files.
 
This is the PR: [Add 2026-04-01-preview API version to Fleet by diyellap · Pull Request #42457 · Azure/azure-rest-ap…](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/42457)
 
Could someone help how to fix this? We compiled first, generated examples, validated examples, ran npx tsv again - This is when all examples get deleted from generated swagger.
 
This is causing typespec validation to fail on the PR.
ground_truth:
This happens when tsv (via tsp compile) doesn’t see any examples at the TypeSpec source location, so the emitter regenerates the Swagger without any x-ms-examples, effectively deleting all examples in the generated OpenAPI.
For Azure REST specs using TypeSpec, the source of truth for examples is not the Swagger folder; it is the examples/<api-version>/ folder located next to your TypeSpec project. tsp compile then copies those example files into the Swagger folder and wires them up in x-ms-examples.
In your case, you:
1.Compiled TypeSpec → generated Swagger.
2.Ran oav generate-examples directly against the Swagger (or otherwise created examples only under the Swagger path).
3.Ran npx tsv again → tsv recompiled the TypeSpec and overwrote the Swagger, but since the examples weren’t present under the TypeSpec examples source folder, they were dropped.
To fix this and get TypeSpec validation passing:
1.Identify your TypeSpec examples source folder for AzureFleet, something like:
specification/azurefleet/AzureFleet.Management/examples/2026-04-01-preview (exact path depends on your project layout, but it will be under the TypeSpec project root, not under resource-manager/.../preview/.../examples).
2.Move/copy your example JSON files into that TypeSpec examples folder. If you generated them with oav from the Swagger folder, copy them from:
specification/azurefleet/resource-manager/Microsoft.AzureFleet/AzureFleet/preview/2026-04-01-preview/examples/ to the corresponding TypeSpec examples folder.
3.Ensure each example file has:
A unique and descriptive title per operation.
The correct operationId matching the operation in your TypeSpec/OpenAPI (you said you already verified this, which is good).
4.Run the standard sequence from the repo root:
```
git clean -xdf   # beware: removes local changes not committed
git pull
npm ci
npx tsv specification/azurefleet/AzureFleet.Management
```
This will:
Compile TypeSpec,
Copy examples from the TypeSpec examples folder to the Swagger folder,
Regenerate azurefleet.json with x-ms-examples wired to those files.
5.Check git status; the only diffs in swagger should be the intended ones. If examples are still missing, double‑check that:
The TypeSpec tspconfig.yaml for AzureFleet is configured with the correct examples directory (follow the Widget sample’s pattern), and
There are no duplicate title + operationId pairs between files (duplicates can cause examples to be dropped).

Once the examples live in the correct TypeSpec source folder and tsp compile manages them, npx tsv will stop deleting them and TypeSpec validation should pass.

testcase: Go SDK prevents union discriminator?
query:
title: Go SDK prevents union discriminator?

question: I'm reviewing a Typespec PR that includes this suppression: 
```
#suppress "@azure-tools/typespec-azure-core/no-string-discriminator" "Discriminator must be plain string for cross-language SDK compatibility (Go emitter does not support extensible unions as discriminator values)."
```
Is this true?
ground_truth:
The justification in that suppression is not accurate, and you should not rely on it.
Azure guidance explicitly prefers extensible unions for discriminators and has a dedicated rule (no-string-discriminator) to enforce this. That rule exists precisely to avoid using a plain string as the discriminator type, so suppressing it on the grounds of SDK compatibility is going against current Azure TypeSpec guidance.
Current Azure client emitters (including Go) are designed to work with the standard @discriminator pattern, and the Azure docs show the discriminator both as a plain string and as an extensible union. There is no official guidance saying that the Go emitter cannot handle extensible unions as discriminator values; on the contrary, the guidance is to use them so adding new discriminator values is non‑breaking.
Therefore:
The statement "Go emitter does not support extensible unions as discriminator values" should not be treated as a general truth or as a standing reason to bypass the rule.
The preferred fix is to change the discriminator property to an extensible union (open enum) rather than suppressing the rule. Only if you hit a concrete, currently-unfixable emitter bug (with a linked issue) should you consider suppression, and then the reason should clearly reference that specific bug rather than a broad claim about Go support.
So: the suppression as written is not well justified and, in normal circumstances, is not necessary. The model should be updated to use an extensible union discriminator instead of a plain string.

testcase: Service-side default not carried over to emitted Python SDK
ground_truth:
The Python signature is intentionally using None instead of the TypeSpec default, and there isn’t a supported way to change that behavior today.
TypeSpec defaults (like force?: boolean = false) are defined as service‑side defaults. The core TypeSpec guidance is that emitters must not turn those into client‑side defaults in SDKs; they should be used by the service implementation when the client omits the value, and reflected in documentation instead of in the client method’s default argument. This is called out explicitly in the emitter docs:
default values are accessed via modelProp.default in emitters
they “SHOULD NOT be used as client-side default values”
they “SHOULD be expressed in documentation to properly communicate the server-side default”
The Python generator follows this rule by:
making the parameter optional (Optional[bool])
using None as the default so the client can distinguish “not passed” from an explicit value
leaving the service default to be communicated through docs rather than via the function signature.
For brownfield cases where an SDK must expose a client‑side default (e.g., to preserve behavior after moving from Swagger to TypeSpec), there is a legacy escape hatch: @Azure.ClientGenerator.Core.Legacy.clientDefaultValue. This is specifically documented as legacy and for backward compatibility, not for new services.
So for a new Foundry data-plane API, the recommended and supported pattern is:
Keep force?: boolean = false in TypeSpec as the service default
Let the emitted Python method use force: Optional[bool] = None
Clearly document in the parameter docstring that the service default is false when omitted.
There isn’t a configuration switch in the current Python emitter to automatically generate force: Optional[bool] = False from the TypeSpec default. Doing so would violate the shared emitter guidance and could also create ambiguity between “unspecified” and “explicitly false” at the client layer.

testcase: What does @identifiers (in Azure.ResourceManager) actually do?
query:
title: What does @identifiers (in Azure.ResourceManager) actually do?

question: What does @identifiers (in Azure.ResourceManager) actually do? Are there side effects, uniqueness constraints, etc?
ground_truth:
In Azure.ResourceManager, @identifiers is purely descriptive metadata used to tell tooling which properties in an array element type are treated as the element’s identifiers. It:
Is applied on an array-typed model property: @identifiers(#["prop1", "prop2"]) items: ItemModel[];
Causes the ARM emitter to generate x-ms-identifiers on that array property in the OpenAPI (or equivalent metadata for other emitters).
It does not by itself:
Enforce runtime or compile-time uniqueness of those properties
Add validation that the values are unique in the array
Change required/optional status of the properties
Change wire format or API behavior beyond the x-ms-identifiers annotation
Any uniqueness or business rules around those identifiers must be enforced by the service or separate validation logic; @identifiers just marks which fields should be considered identifiers for that array element type by ARM and client tooling.

testcase: When using @added() on a required property
query:
title: When using @added() on a required property

question: When using @added() on a required property, does TSP automatically make this property nullable on the models for older API versions?
ground_truth:
No. When you use @added() on a required property, TypeSpec does not retroactively change anything for older API versions.
Behavior is:
The property simply does not exist at all in versions before the @added version (it is neither required nor optional there).
Starting from the version you pass to @added, the property appears exactly as you declare it (required if you wrote prop: T;, optional if you wrote prop?: T;).
If you need a property to exist in all versions but change from required to optional (or vice versa), use @madeOptional / @madeRequired instead.

it's not entirely true that @added does nothing to previous versions.  If you add @added(Versions.v2)it makes the property not appear in versions before v2.  Other than the @removed decorator, all typespec versioning decorators fundamentally make changes on versions before the change  This is because the state of types in the spec most closely matches the latest version of the API.

testcase: Mix of GA ready and preview endpoints
query:
title: Mix of GA ready and preview endpoints

question: We have endpoints for Start,Deallocate and hibernate VMs as well as create VMs on our RP, our Start, Deallocate ,hibernate endpoints are GA ready and client is waiting for a stable SDK version to start using that in production. 
but our Create endpoint is still in preview stage and we expect changes there as more data comes out of private preview. 
What is recommended way to expose start, deallocate and hibernate endpoints as GA ready and get stable SDK for it, while keeping Create in private preview, can all these endpoints be part of same type spec PR 
start, deallocate and hibernate --> stable version 2026-06-06
create --> 2026-06-06-preview or 2026-07-06-preview ? 
 
second question can all these be added as part of same PR ? and then can we request SDK only for stable version ?
ground_truth:
Generally the main branch of the public repo is meant for APIs that will generate SDKs.  If you have a preview version that you don't want to generate SDKs for, then you should not include it in main.
You should not use the same API date for a preview and stable version, dates should be montonically increasing, so 2026-07-06-preview would be appropriate if the previous stable were 2026-06-06.
Your PR will need to go through ARM review, not sure of the details, but it sounds like you might be missing required lifecycle operations for your resources, which ARM would have to approve, if true
If you have not done so, you should onboard to the SDK team, and have a discussion about releasing your API : https://aka.ms/azsdk/join

testcase: @secret in POST operation response
query:
title: @secret in POST operation response

question: We have a property returned by a POST operation response that was required by validation to use @secret this returns values user needs to use in their application to use certain feature of our service.  Since the values are sensitive we return it as response of a POST action and not in any GET response. This I belive is in compliance with the RPC
Just to clarify, can /will this value be masked/sanitized in API POST response or generated SDKs ?
ground_truth:
If you decorate a property with @secret in TypeSpec, this does NOT mean the value will be masked or sanitized in the API POST response or in generated SDKs. The @secret decorator is used to indicate that the property contains sensitive information (such as passwords, keys, tokens, credentials, or other secrets) and is required by Azure ARM guidelines for proper identification and handling of secrets.
The actual value will be returned in the POST response as specified by your API contract. The @secret decorator ensures that the property is marked as sensitive in the OpenAPI spec (via x-ms-secret), which informs tooling, SDKs, and Azure services to treat it with extra care (e.g., not logging it, not exposing it in GET responses, and possibly prompting SDKs to handle it securely). However, it does NOT automatically mask or sanitize the value in the response payload.
Your approach—returning the secret only in the POST response and not in GET responses—is fully compliant with Azure API guidelines. If you want to mask or sanitize the value, you must implement that logic in your service backend. TypeSpec and the generated OpenAPI/SDKs will not do this automatically.
